agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposed change to auto-delete delivery records after 90 days, down from 7 years, framed as infrastructure optimization. Platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's legal compliance posture, contractual obligations, and data governance policy with potentially catastrophic business consequences.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Record Lifecycle & Data Governance",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete audit trail of a delivery including proof of delivery, timestamps, recipient confirmation, chain of custody, and associated financial transaction data." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "The legally significant evidence that a consignment was delivered to the correct recipient at a specific time and location. Often required in disputes, chargebacks, and regulatory audits." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The enforceable rules governing how long specific categories of data are stored, based on legal obligations, contractual commitments, and business needs — NOT storage cost alone." },
      { "term": "B2B Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The use of delivery records by business customers to satisfy their own regulatory, tax, and audit obligations. Our data is an input to their compliance chain." },
      { "term": "Right to Rectification/Erasure", "definition": "GDPR Articles 16-17 rights that govern when data MUST be deleted, distinct from when data MAY be deleted. Retention obligations override erasure requests when a legal basis exists." },
      { "term": "Statutory Retention Period", "definition": "The legally mandated minimum period that certain records must be kept, which varies by jurisdiction and record type. Cannot be shortened by internal policy." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records that constitute tax-relevant documentation (invoices, proof of supply of goods, VAT evidence) must be retained for the statutory minimum period: 6 years in the UK (HMRC), 10 years in some EU member states (e.g., France, Italy).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a legal obligation imposed by national tax authorities. Violation exposes the company to fines, failed audits, and potential criminal liability. No VP or CTO can override statutory law.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record containing tax-relevant data is deleted before the applicable statutory retention period for its jurisdiction has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Proof of Delivery records must be retained for the duration of the limitation period for contract disputes: 6 years in the UK (Limitation Act 1980), typically 3-5 years in most EU jurisdictions, up to 10 years in some.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Deleting POD within the dispute limitation window eliminates the company's ability to defend against claims of non-delivery, which is an existential legal risk. A claimant could sue for non-delivery and the company would have no evidence to counter the claim.",
      "testable_assertion": "No Proof of Delivery record is deleted while the applicable limitation period for contractual disputes in its jurisdiction is still open."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "B2B customer delivery records must be retained for at least the period contractually agreed with each B2B customer, which typically aligns with or exceeds the customer's own statutory compliance obligations.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Premature deletion of B2B customer data constitutes a breach of contract and destroys data that B2B customers depend on for their own regulatory compliance. This creates cascading legal liability.",
      "testable_assertion": "No B2B delivery record is deleted before the contractually agreed retention period with that B2B customer has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "GDPR and UK GDPR require that personal data is not retained longer than necessary for its purpose, but also recognize that legal obligations constitute a valid basis for retention beyond the data subject's preference.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR compliance is a legal obligation. Both over-retention and under-retention can be violations. The retention period must be justified per data category, not set as a single blanket duration.",
      "testable_assertion": "Each category of data within a delivery record has a documented legal basis and defined retention period. Deletion occurs per-category when each basis expires, not as a blanket 90-day wipe."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Consumer dispute resolution (chargebacks, complaints, ombudsman cases) requires access to delivery records. Chargeback windows can extend to 540 days (Visa/Mastercard) and ombudsman complaints can be filed years after the event.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Chargeback windows are set by payment networks and could theoretically change, but they currently far exceed 90 days. This is a business policy dependency rather than a physical constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Delivery records linked to financial transactions are retained for at least the maximum chargeback window (currently 540 days) plus a reasonable buffer."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A B2B logistics customer is audited by their national tax authority 4 years after deliveries occurred and requests delivery records from our platform as evidence.", "expected_behavior": "Records must be available. Under the 90-day policy they would have been destroyed 3.75 years earlier, making our B2B customer non-compliant and us in breach of contract." },
    { "scenario": "A consumer disputes a high-value delivery 8 months after it occurred, claiming non-receipt, and initiates a chargeback.", "expected_behavior": "The Proof of Delivery must be retrievable to contest the chargeback. Under 90-day deletion, the POD is gone and the company must absorb the loss with no recourse." },
    { "scenario": "HMRC or an EU tax authority audits the platform's VAT records 5 years after the tax period.", "expected_behavior": "All delivery records constituting evidence of supply of goods must be producible. Under 90-day deletion, the company cannot comply with the audit, resulting in penalties and potential prosecution." },
    { "scenario": "A data subject exercises their GDPR right to erasure on day 30.", "expected_behavior": "The platform must evaluate whether a legal basis for retention exists (tax, contractual obligation). If so, erasure is lawfully refused for those categories. A blanket 90-day policy does not replace per-request lawful basis evaluation." },
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer churns but their delivery data is deleted 90 days later. Two years later, a legal dispute arises about deliveries made during the contract.", "expected_behavior": "Records must still exist for the duration of the limitation period regardless of customer status. Deletion after 90 days destroys evidence needed for litigation defense." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE NEGATIVE. (1) Tax authority penalties for failure to produce records during audit — fines can reach millions in EU/UK. (2) Inability to defend chargebacks — direct revenue loss on every disputed delivery older than 90 days. (3) B2B contract breach claims and potential loss of enterprise customers. (4) Estimated storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory fine or lost enterprise contract. The 'optimization' creates catastrophic tail risk to save marginal infrastructure cost.",
    "operational": "SEVERELY DISRUPTIVE. (1) Customer support loses ability to investigate complaints older than 90 days. (2) Analytics, trend analysis, and route optimization lose historical training data. (3) B2B customers lose API access to their historical delivery data, triggering support escalations and churn. (4) Legal and compliance teams lose the ability to respond to regulatory inquiries.",
    "user": "NEGATIVE for both segments. B2C consumers lose recourse for disputes after 90 days. B2B customers lose access to data they depend on for their own compliance reporting — this is a service-breaking change for them, likely triggering contract exit clauses.",
    "risk": "CRITICAL. This change exposes the company to: (1) Regulatory enforcement action under tax law in multiple jurisdictions. (2) GDPR enforcement action for inadequate retention policy documentation (blanket policy without per-category legal basis analysis). (3) Breach of contract claims from B2B customers. (4) Inability to defend litigation for 6+ years of potential claims. (5) Loss of enterprise customer trust and revenue. The risk profile is asymmetric: small cost savings vs. existential legal and commercial exposure.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "B2B Logistics Customers", "impact": "Critical — lose access to delivery data needed for their own tax and regulatory compliance. Likely contract breach. High churn risk." },
      { "group": "B2C Consumers", "impact": "High — lose ability to dispute deliveries or request proof of delivery after 90 days. Reduced consumer protection." },
      { "group": "Finance / Tax Team", "impact": "Critical — cannot produce records for tax audits. Company becomes non-compliant with HMRC and EU tax authorities." },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance Team", "impact": "Critical — cannot defend litigation, respond to regulatory inquiries, or produce discovery documents." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "High — cannot investigate or resolve complaints for any delivery older than 90 days." },
      { "group": "Data / Analytics Team", "impact": "Medium — loses historical data for route optimization, demand forecasting, and performance analysis." },
      { "group": "Engineering / Infrastructure", "impact": "Low positive — marginal reduction in storage costs, which is the sole stated benefit." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 92,
    "reasoning": "The regulatory requirements for record retention in EU/UK tax law and the contractual obligations to B2B customers are well-established and not ambiguous. UK HMRC 6-year retention, EU member state requirements up to 10 years, and limitation periods for contract disputes are publicly documented law. The only uncertainty is the exact contractual terms with specific B2B customers, which would need to be verified. The core conclusion — that 90-day blanket deletion violates multiple legal obligations — is high confidence.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK HMRC requires 6-year retention of tax-relevant records",
      "EU member states require 6-10 year retention of tax-relevant records",
      "Limitation periods for contract disputes exceed 90 days in all EU/UK jurisdictions",
      "B2B customers depend on delivery data for their own compliance",
      "Chargeback windows exceed 90 days"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact contractual retention commitments with specific B2B customers — need contract review",
      "Whether delivery records are currently classified as tax-relevant documents in the platform's data classification scheme",
      "Specific EU member state retention periods vary and would need per-market legal review"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Delivery records contain or are linked to financial/tax-relevant data (invoices, VAT, proof of supply)",
      "B2B customer contracts include data availability SLAs or retention commitments",
      "The platform has not already separated tax-critical data into a separate retention tier"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' but fundamentally alters the platform's legal compliance posture, contractual obligations, and ability to serve both B2B and B2C customers. Data retention policy IS business logic in a regulated logistics platform — it is not infrastructure. This framing contradiction must be resolved before any implementation proceeds.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["B2B Logistics Customers", "Finance/Tax Team", "Legal/Compliance Team", "B2C Consumers", "Executive Leadership"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject the 90-day blanket deletion entirely and maintain 7-year retention",
        "B: Implement tiered retention with per-category legal basis analysis — e.g., anonymize PII after purpose expires but retain tax and POD records for statutory periods",
        "C: Implement cold storage tiering to reduce costs while maintaining full retention — move records older than 90 days to cheaper archive storage"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C for immediate cost relief, followed by Option B for long-term GDPR-compliant data lifecycle management. Option A (current state) is acceptable but doesn't address the legitimate cost concern. The 90-day blanket deletion (the proposal) is NOT an acceptable option."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "90-day deletion violates statutory tax record retention requirements in UK (6 years) and EU member states (6-10 years)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" },
    { "violation": "90-day deletion destroys Proof of Delivery within the limitation period for contractual disputes (3-6+ years), eliminating the company's ability to defend claims", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "90-day deletion likely breaches B2B customer contracts that depend on delivery data availability for their own compliance obligations", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "Blanket 90-day deletion without per-category legal basis analysis does not constitute a lawful GDPR retention policy", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" },
    { "violation": "90-day deletion destroys records within the chargeback dispute window (up to 540 days), causing unrecoverable financial losses on disputed transactions", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" }
  ]
}
